title-group.article-title: Investments in photoreceptors compete with investments in optics to determine eye design

institution-wrap.institution: Department of Zoology, University of Cambridge

event.event-desc: This manuscript was published as a preprint.
This manuscript was published as a reviewed preprint.

license.license-p: This article is distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits unrestricted use and redistribution provided that the original author and source are credited.

abstract.p: Eyes provide opportunities to understand the function, design, development, and evolution of elaborate sense organs. We take a new cost–benefit approach to understanding eye design by considering that optics and photoreceptors compete for the resources invested in an integrated system. We investigate this competition theoretically and empirically using a new measure of cost, specific volume. This common currency for optics and photoreceptors relates investments to image quality via geometrical, optical, and physiological constraints. By covering the morphospace of an eye of given type and cost, we model how trading optics against photoreceptors changes information capacity. In apposition compound eyes and simple eyes, an optimum configuration maximises efficiency. Efficiency requires heavy investment in photoreceptors and depends on photoreceptor energy consumption. Optimum information capacities and efficiencies scale non-linearly with total investment. Diurnal insects’ apposition eyes follow trends that promote efficiency: photoreceptor arrays take 40–80% of total specific volume, photoreceptor length increases systematically with spatial resolution, and photoreceptors are exceptionally long. Thus, competition between optics and photoreceptors shapes eye design, and matching investments in optics and photoreceptors to improve efficiency is a design principle. Our new methodology can be developed to view the adaptive radiation of eyes through a cost–benefit lens.

abstract.title: eLife digest
abstract.p: Animal eyes come in many shapes and sizes, from simple eye pits lined with light receptors to complex structures with bent lenses and corneas, as well as compound eyes. Nonetheless, the underlying principle is the same: an optical system forms an image that is captured by a photoreceptor array.

Over millions of years, eyes have adapted to an organism’s lifestyle and habitat and can therefore vary not only between species but also between sexes and developmental stages. For example, fast-flying predators such as dragonflies (with compound eyes) and hawks (with simple eyes) have larger eyes with larger lenses, enabling them to resolve finer spatial detail. However, although larger eyes generally perform better, most eyes remain relatively small because they are energetically expensive organs. Thus, eye design reflects a balance between costs and benefits.

Previous studies of eye design have largely considered costs and benefits separately, overlooking an important interaction: optics and photoreceptors compete for limited resources. Greater investment in optics improves image quality and information projected onto photoreceptors, while greater investment in photoreceptors enhances the system’s ability to capture that information. This raises a key question: is this competition a significant factor in eye design?

Heras and Laughlin addressed this question by introducing a mathematical framework that links investment directly to performance. Their approach allows modelling of how information capture changes as resources shift between optics and photoreceptors.

Using mathematical models of two types of compound eyes and one simple eye, they showed that all eye types studied possess an optimal allocation of resources that maximizes information capture. They further demonstrated that the energy costs of photoreceptors play a critical role in shaping an optimized eye. Applying their model to insect compound eyes, they estimated how resources are distributed between optics and photoreceptors and found that, as predicted, insects invest efficiently by allocating more resources to photoreceptors than to optics. Such an alignment of investments to maximize efficiency thus represents a new principle of eye design.

Research on how sensory systems are adapted to an organism’s lifestyle and habitat has largely focused on benefits rather than costs. The method developed by Heras and Laughlin provides a powerful tool for understanding how cost-benefit relationships have shaped the evolution of eyes, brains and behavior. Further progress will depend on improved measurements of costs. Their work also suggests that the remarkable energy efficiency of brains may arise from a similar balancing of cost–benefit functions across their component systems.

funding-group.funding-statement: The funders had no role in study design, data collection, and interpretation, or the decision to submit the work for publication.

custom-meta.meta-value: Allocating space, materials and energy to an eye's optics and photoreceptor array is a major factor in eye design that explains obvious differences between simple eyes and compound eyes.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The design of eyes has long fascinated biologists with its variations, innovations, numerous adaptations of form to function, and insights into the evolution of organs, both rudimentary and perfectly contrived (Darwin, 1859; Land and Fernald, 1992; Nilsson and Land, 2012; Oakley and Speiser, 2015; Walls, 1942). We address a question of eye design that has not been considered before, but is familiar to people who construct imaging systems on tight budgets. How should the resources invested in an eye be divided between an eye’s two major components, the optical system that forms images and the photoreceptor array that captures images?

The benefits of enlarging the dioptric apparatus and the photoreceptor array are well understood; consequently, numerous studies relate measures of eye morphology (radius, width, length, lens diameter, pupil area, and photoreceptor length) to optical, geometrical, and physiological constraints on performance to show how morphology adapts an eye to lifestyle and habitat (Barlow, 1952; Kirschfeld, 1976; Land, 1981; Nilsson and Land, 2012; Niven et al., 2007; Snyder, 1979; Labhart and Nilsson, 1995; Land, 1997; Thomas et al., 2020). However, the fact that an eye’s performance depends on two systems that effectively compete for the resources invested in an eye, the dioptric apparatus and the photoreceptor array, is rarely considered (Kirschfeld, 1976; Kröger and Biehlmaier, 2009). We investigate how trade-offs between dioptrics and photoreceptor array determine an eye’s performance and efficiency by developing a new cost–benefit approach, which uses measures of eye morphology to calculate costs, and relates these measures to optical, geometrical, and physiological constraints to calculate benefits.

We consider three basic eye types, two types of apposition compound eye, neural superposition (NS) and fused rhabdom, and a simple eye (Figure 1). We establish and road test our cost–benefit approach using apposition compound eyes because they offer a unique combination of advantages. The optical and physiological constraints are well documented, their effects on performance are measured and modelled (Snyder, 1979; Stavenga, 2004b; Howard et al., 1987; Hardie and Postma, 2008; Song et al., 2012; Heras and Laughlin, 2017) and their influence on eye design is well established. For example, the compound eyes of houseflies, praying mantises, dragonflies, and robber flies have foveas for detecting, locating, and catching flying mates or prey. Here, the facet lens is enlarged to reduce the diffraction limit to optical resolving power, the ommatidia are more densely packed to increase angular resolution, and photoreceptors are elongated to increase signal to noise ratio by transducing more photons (Rossel, 1979; Hardie, 1985; Labhart and Nilsson, 1995; Burton and Laughlin, 2003; Wardill et al., 2017). In addition, there are clear indications that photoreceptors are costly and investments in optics and photoreceptors are matched. Apposition eyes’ photoreceptor arrays obviously fill a substantial fraction of eye volume because photoreceptors construct long photoreceptive waveguides (rhabdomeres, rhabdoms), substantial photoreceptor energy costs increase with rhabdomere length (Niven et al., 2007), and there is a precise, fine scale covariation of acuity and photoreceptor length, measured in two apposition eyes (Labhart and Nilsson, 1995; Rossel, 1979) and obvious in many others (e.g. Figure 1d).

caption.title: Eye structure and geometry define resolution and costs.
caption.p: (a) Fused rhabdom apposition eye, photoreceptors coding a pixel form fused rhabdom and send axons to a single neural module; (b) neural superposition (NS) apposition eye, photoreceptor forms its own rhabdomere, photoreceptors with same optical axes code single pixel and send axons to single neural module; and (c) simple eye, as in a camera each photoreceptor codes a single pixel. (d) Gradient of investment in spatial acuity: apposition eye, honeybee drone, Apis mellifera. From ventral to dorsal, lens diameter D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} increases and interommatidial angle Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} decreases to increase spatial resolution, and rhabdom length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, increases to increase SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document}. 10 μm thick longitudinal section, (DA) dorsal eye area and (VA) ventral area. BM – retina’s basement membrane; E – equator separating dorsal and ventral regions. From Menzel et al., 1991, original micrograph courtesy of Doekele Stavenga. (e) Schematic section of locally spherical apposition eye region. Volumes of optics and photoreceptor array are determined by dimensions that constrain the quality of the spatial image coded by photoreceptors: lens diameter D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, focal distance f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document}, interommatidial angle Δϕ=D/R\begin{document}$\Delta\phi=D/R$\end{document} where R\begin{document}$R$\end{document} is eye radius, and rhabdom(ere) length L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}.

sec.p: Our new cost–benefit approach establishes that photoreceptor cost is a major factor in eye design that influences optics by competing for the resources invested in an eye. Consequently, matching investments in optics and photoreceptors to improve efficiency is a principle of eye design. This principle can explain why apposition eyes have longer photoreceptors than simple eyes and photoreceptor length increases with spatial acuity in apposition and simple eyes. We suggest that our approach can advance our understanding of visual ecology by costing the benefits of the adaptations observed in eyes (Cronin et al., 2014; Nilsson and Land, 2012). Moreover, because the allocation of resources to optics and photoreceptors both promotes cost-effectiveness and depends upon developmental and physiological mechanisms (Casares and McGregor, 2021; Niven and Laughlin, 2008), our cost–benefit approach may well contribute to our understanding of how and why eyes evolve (Nilsson and Pelger, 1994).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: We start with the neural superposition (NS) eyes of flies. This type of apposition compound eye provides both the detailed analyses of optical and physiological constraints required to develop a model that relates investments in optics and photoreceptor arrays to performance, and the published data on dioptrics and photoreceptor arrays required to test our model. Moreover, because fly NS eyes vary in size between species while retaining similar structure and function, we simplify our study by developing and using a generic fly NS model. We then model and examine fused rhabdom apposition eyes to identify general principles of apposition eye design. Finally, we model a simple (camera) eye to demonstrate that changing eye type changes the distribution of costs. In all three types, we consider diurnal eyes operating in daylight when the number of available photons is not limiting. Although this restriction eliminates the many ways in which eyes are designed to operate efficiently at lower light levels (Nilsson and Land, 2012), it makes it easier to establish points of principle by reducing the numbers of variables in our models. The symbols we use for variables and parameters are listed in Table 1.

caption.title: Symbols used in the Results and the Discussion.

tr.td: Diameter of rhabdom (fused-rhabdom eye) or rhabdomere (NS eye)
Radius of eye or locally spherical eye region
Length of rhabdom or rhabdomere, also depth of photoreceptor array
Refractive index of eye’s internal medium
Half width of lens point-spread function
Number of microvilli per unit length of rhabdom or rhabdomere

sec.title: Calculating the benefits and costs of investing in optics
sec.p: In daylight, when the number of available photons is not limiting, investments in optics buy increased optical contrast and spatial resolving power by reducing optical and geometrical constraints, as follows. An apposition eye forms and captures images with an array of ommatidia (Figure 1). Each ommatidium uses a facet lens of diameter, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, and focal length (in air), f\begin{document}$f$\end{document}, to focus light onto the entrance aperture of photoreceptive waveguides, a single fused rhabdom or several rhabdomeres (Figure 1) of diameter drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document} . Increasing D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} sharpens a photoreceptor’s angular sensitivity by reducing the blur produced by the Airy disk diffraction pattern, whose half-width Δρl=λ/D\begin{document}$\Delta\rho_{l}=\lambda/D$\end{document} radians, where λ\begin{document}$\lambda$\end{document} is the wavelength of light, taken to be 500 nm. Increasing f\begin{document}$f$\end{document} and reducing drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document} also sharpen angular sensitivity by reducing the angular subtense of the photoreceptor entrance aperture, drh/f\begin{document}$d_{rh}/f$\end{document}. Sharpening angular sensitivity has two benefits: it increases spatial resolving power and increases image contrast.

We calculate the effects of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document}, and f\begin{document}$f$\end{document} on the half-width of a fly photoreceptor’s angular sensitivity, Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document}, using two formulae, Snyder’s simplification, his convolution of Gaussians (CoG) Δρ2=Δρl2+Δρrh2\begin{document}$\Delta\rho^{2}=\Delta\rho_{l}^{2}+\Delta\rho_{rh}^{2}$\end{document}, where Δρl=λ/D\begin{document}$\Delta\rho_{l}=\lambda/D$\end{document} and Δρrh=drh/f\begin{document}$\Delta\rho_{rh}=d_{rh}/f$\end{document}; and Stavenga’s better approximation, Δρ=1.26Δρl\begin{document}$\Delta\rho=1.26\Delta\rho_{l}$\end{document}, obtained using a comprehensive wave-optics model (WOM) of flies’ NS eyes operating in bright light (see Methods for more details). For CoG, we fix drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document} = 1.9 μm because this value is typical of fly R1–6 (Hardie, 1985). CoG provides a lower bound to optical resolving power and WOM a more efficient upper bound that, in bright light, makes better use of investments in optics by operating closer to the diffraction limit.

The benefits of investing in optics come at a cost: increasing D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and lengthening f\begin{document}$f$\end{document} to reduce Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document} expands the volume of the dioptric apparatus, Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document}, which in apposition eyes is easily calculated from eye geometry (Figure 1e). D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} define the radius R=D/Δϕ\begin{document}$R=D/\Delta\phi$\end{document} of a locally spherical eye region, in which the dioptric apparatus (lens, cone, and screening pigment) is a shell of thickness f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document}, the focal distance from lens front surface to focal plane. Thus,(1)Vo=13[R3−(R−f′)3]µm3sr−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{o}=\frac{1}{3}[R^{3}-(R-f^{\prime})^{3}] \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1}.$$\end{document}

Because Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document} is a specific measure, volume per unit solid angle of visual space, we can compare investments in eye regions that differ in radius and angular extent. Thus, Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document} handles the regional variations in R\begin{document}$R$\end{document} observed in many compound eyes.

To simplify our expression for Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document} (Equation 1) we fix lens F-number, F=f/D\begin{document}$F=f/D$\end{document} and apply the formula for image formation by a convex cornea, f′=f.ni\begin{document}$f^{\prime}=f.n_{i}$\end{document}, where ni\begin{document}$n_{i}$\end{document} is the refractive index of the internal medium (Nilsson and Land, 2012). This formula describes several apposition eyes, including fly NS eyes (Rossel, 1979; Stavenga et al., 1990; Stavenga, 2003a; Zeil, 1983). Thus,(2)Vo=13[R3−(R−FniD)3]µm3sr−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{o}=\frac{1}{3}[R^{3}-(R-Fn_{i}D)^{3}] \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1}.$$\end{document}
We use values measured in blowfly, F=2.0\begin{document}$F=2.0$\end{document} (Stavenga, 2003a) and ni\begin{document}$n_{i}$\end{document}, = 1.34 (Seitz, 1968).

Fixing F\begin{document}$F$\end{document} both simplifies our models by reducing the number of free parameters and accounts for the dependence of the photon flux entering an ommatidium on D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} by making image brightness (the flux per unit area of rhabdomere entrance aperture) independent of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}. We use the specific volume of optics, Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document}, as our measure of the cost of optics, Co\begin{document}$C_{o}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1. This usage assumes that the cost of materials, the cost of metabolic energy for maintaining function, and the energy cost of carriage all increase in equal proportion to volume, in all parts of the dioptric apparatus, namely the corneal lens, cone, and surrounding pigment cells. Given the lack of definitive data on composition and costs, this assumption is a reasonable starting point (Discussion).

sec.title: Calculating the benefits and costs of investing in photoreceptors
sec.p: Investing in photoreceptors buys photoreceptor signal-to-noise ratio, SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document}, by reducing the effects of an optical constraint, the Poisson statistics of photon absorption (Nilsson and Land, 2012), and at higher light levels a physiological constraint, the saturation of a photoreceptor’s transduction units – its light sensitive microvilli (Heras and Laughlin, 2017; Howard et al., 1987; Song et al., 2012). A microvillus contains rhodopsin molecules, the intermediate molecules of the phototransduction cascade and the ion channels they activate, and it uses these to generate a brief pulse of depolarising current, a quantum bump, following the activation of a single rhodopsin molecule by an absorbed photon. This all-or-none response takes time to complete, during which the microvillus cannot respond to the absorption of another photon (Hardie and Postma, 2008; Song and Juusola, 2014). Therefore, as light intensity rises to daylight levels, SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document} falls below the Poisson limit because a photoreceptor’s transduction units saturate (Howard et al., 1987). At any given time, a significant fraction of microvilli are failing to respond to the absorption of a photon by rhodopsin because they are already engaged in processing a bump. Consequently, the transduction rate ψ\begin{document}$\psi$\end{document} no longer increases linearly with the absorption rate, and bump statistics change from Poisson to binomial. In daylight, flies activate a photomechanical response, a longitudinal pupil, to prevent excessive saturation and maintain SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document} close to the maximum permitted by binomial statistics,(3)SNRph=12Nvil,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle SNR_{ph} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{N_{vil}} \,,$$\end{document}

where SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document} is defined with respect to a signal of unit contrast (Howard et al., 1987). Note that by operating at this upper limit, the fly obtains maximum benefit from investing in Nvil\begin{document}$N_{vil}$\end{document}. Thus, the benefit of investing in a longer rhabdomere is an increase in SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document} in bright light, according to(4)SNRph=12νL,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle SNR_{ph} =\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\nu L} \,,$$\end{document}

where L egin{document}$L$\end{document} is rhabdomere length and ν egin{document}$
u$\end{document} is the number of microvilli per unit length. We assume ν egin{document}$
u$\end{document} is constant along the rhabdomere and from published results we estimate ν=230µ⁢m-1 egin{document}$
u=$\end{document} 230 µm−1 (Methods). We acknowledge that diameter, cross section, and taper often vary within and among rhabdomeres. We are fixing ν egin{document}$
u$\end{document} at a plausible value for a generic model.

The cost of increasing SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document} by increasing L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} is easily calculated. In fly NS eyes, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} is also the depth of the photoreceptor array because the rhabdomeres of the six largest photoreceptors, R1–6, stretch from the focal plane of the lens to the basement membrane (Hardie, 1985). Thus in a locally spherical eye region (Figure 1) the photoreceptor array’s specific volume is(5)Vph=13[(R−f′)3−(R−f′−L)3]µm3sr−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{ph}=\frac{1}{3}[(R-f^{\prime})^{3}-(R-f^{\prime}-L)^{3}] \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1}.$$\end{document}

Substituting f′=F.ni.D\begin{document}$f^{\prime}=F.n_{i}.D$\end{document}(6)Vph=13[(R−FniD)3−(R−FniD−L)3]µm3sr−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{ph}=\frac{1}{3}[(R-Fn_{i}D)^{3}-(R-Fn_{i}D-L)^{3}] \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1}.$$\end{document}

According to eye geometry (Figure 1), the total specific volume of an eye region is(7)Vtot=Vo+Vphµm3sr−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle V_{tot}=V_{o}+V_{ph} \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1}.$$\end{document}

Observe that because both Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document} (Equations 1 and 2) and Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} (Equation 5; Equation 6) depend upon R\begin{document}$R$\end{document} and R=D/Δϕ\begin{document}$R=D/\Delta\phi$\end{document}, both Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document} and Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} increase with the number of ommatidia per unit solid angle.

For the photoreceptor array, we assume that the costs of space, materials and energy for carriage increase in proportion to volume with the same constants of proportionality as optics. Again, in the absence of measurements, this assumption is a reasonable starting point (Discussion). However, this assumption breaks down when we consider specific metabolic rates. To enable and power phototransduction, a photoreceptor has an exceptionally high specific metabolic rate (energy consumed per gram, and hence unit volume, per second) (Laughlin et al., 1998; Niven et al., 2007; Pangršič et al., 2005). We account for this extra cost by applying an energy surcharge, SE\begin{document}$S_{E}$\end{document}. To equate with our other estimates of cost, this surcharge has units of μm3 sr−1, and we assume that it increases in proportion to Nvil\begin{document}$N_{vil}$\end{document} because Nvil\begin{document}$N_{vil}$\end{document} determines the magnitude of the photoreceptor’s light-gated conductance (Hardie and Postma, 2008; Heras and Laughlin, 2017). We satisfy these two requirements by defining(8)SE=KENvilµm3sr−1,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle S_{E}=K_{E}N_{vil} \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1},$$\end{document}
where KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, the photoreceptor energy tariff, converts the energy consumed by a photoreceptor per microvillus into an equivalent volume.

We estimate KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} (Methods) by adopting the method used to estimate the energy cost of weapons carried by flying beetles (Goyens et al., 2015). We divide the energy consumed per microvillus by the animal’s mass specific metabolic rate to obtain an equivalent body mass which, assuming a density of 1.0, is our volume equivalent, KEµ⁢m3\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} per microvillus. KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} is at present poorly determined: it depends upon several ecological, physiological and behavioural factors, it will vary among species and among individuals of the same species, and there is barely enough data (Methods). Given this uncertainty, we model a range of values from KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} = 0 to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} = 0.64 that covers the range we estimate for blowfly, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} = 0.13 to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} = 0.52 (Methods).
To summarise costs, for the photoreceptor array(9)Cph=Vph+SEµm3sr−1,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{ph}=V_{ph}+S_{E} \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1},$$\end{document}
and for the optics(10)Co=Voµm3sr−1,\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{o}=V_{o} \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1},$$\end{document}

Because Co\begin{document}$C_{o}$\end{document} and Cph\begin{document}$C_{ph}$\end{document} have the same units and are specific (per unit solid angle), we can transfer resources between optics and photoreceptors within the constraint of total specific cost(11)Ctot=Co+Cphµm3sr−1.\begin{document}$$\displaystyle C_{tot}=C_{o}+C_{ph} \,\mathrm{µm}^3\,{\rm sr}^{-1}.$$\end{document}

sec.title: Modelling the effects of resource allocation on NS eye performance
sec.p: When Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} is fixed, allocating a smaller proportion to optics and a larger proportion to photoreceptors, trades the benefits of investing in optics for the benefits of investing in photoreceptors (Figure 2).

caption.title: The effect of trading investment in photoreceptor array for investment in dioptric apparatus.
caption.p: The schematic shows two eye regions of equal specific volume, the left investing more heavily in photoreceptors, the right more heavily on optics. The images they capture show that transferring resources from photoreceptor array to optics increases image sharpness and contrast at the expense of increasing noise.

sec.p: Having used constraints to link investments in dioptrics and photoreceptor array to three major determinants of the quality of the achromatic images captured by fly photoreceptors R1–6, namely Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, and SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document}, we can now model how trade-offs between optics and photoreceptors change an eye’s ability to support vision. Several measures of visual performance depend on Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, and SNRph\begin{document}$S\,N\,R_{ph}$\end{document}. To establish proof of principle, we use a general measure that embraces a wide variety of resolvable achromatic image details – spatio-temporal information capacity in bits sr−1 s−1. Information capacity has proved useful for measuring the performance of compound eyes (de Ruyter van Steveninck and Laughlin, 1996), discovering design principles (van Hateren, 1992; Howard and Snyder, 1983; Snyder et al., 1977), and illuminating the evolution of simple eyes (Nilsson and Pelger, 1994). The measure is particularly relevant for a fly NS eye because coding by second order neurons is adapted to maximise information capacity (van Hateren, 1992; Laughlin, 1981).

To model the effects of resource allocation on information capacity, we fix Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and generate pairs of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} that cover the range of values permitted by eye geometry. Each D,L\begin{document}$D,L$\end{document} pair specifies an investment in optics, Co(D,L)\begin{document}$C_{o}(D,L)$\end{document}, an investment in photoreceptors Cph(D,L)\begin{document}$C_{ph}(D,L)$\end{document} and hence the three determinants of image quality, Δρ(D,L)\begin{document}$\Delta\rho(D,L)$\end{document}, Δϕ(D,L)\begin{document}$\Delta\phi(D,L)$\end{document}, and SNRph(D,L)\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}(D,L)$\end{document}, that specify spatio-temporal information capacity H(D,L)\begin{document}$H(D,L)$\end{document} bits sr−1 s−1.

We calculate H(D,L)\begin{document}$H(D,L)$\end{document} (Methods) in the frequency domain (Howard and Snyder, 1983; van Hateren, 1992). In brief, a 2D power spectrum typical of natural scenes is low-pass filtered by the photoreceptor angular sensitivity function, Δρ(D,L)\begin{document}$\Delta\rho(D,L)$\end{document}, and sampled by a hexagonal lattice of photoreceptors specified by Δϕ(D,L)\begin{document}$\Delta\phi(D,L)$\end{document}. Movement of the retinal image modulates the flux of photons entering a photoreceptor by converting spatial frequencies into temporal frequencies, according to the distribution of angular velocities generated during behaviour. Then, during transduction, the photoreceptors low-pass filter these temporal frequencies and add the noise generated by sampling the photon flux stochastically with transduction units (microvilli). We use (van Hateren, 1992) formulae for the 1/f2\begin{document}$1/f^{2}$\end{document} power spectrum of natural scenes and the distribution of image velocities appropriate for blowfly, and we temporally low-pass filter according to the measured properties of a fully light-adapted blowfly photoreceptor R1–6 (Methods). We also modify van Hateren’s method to take account of the spatial aliasing that occurs when an array of ommatidia undersamples an image (Methods).

By calculating information capacities for each D,L\begin{document}$D,L$\end{document} pair, we define the performance surface H(D,L)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{H(D,\,L)}$\end{document} that covers the morphospace of model eyes of given F-number, F\begin{document}$F$\end{document}, and specific cost, Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. The performance surface is a flat-topped ridge with steep flanks (Figure 3). Atop the ridge, a single point of maximum capacity, H(Dopt,Lopt)\begin{document}$H(D_{opt},L_{opt})$\end{document}, sits in an extensive zone within which capacity, and hence efficiency, is >95%\begin{document}$ \gt 95\%$\end{document} maximum (red zone in Figure 3). With efficiency dropping steeply away from the ridge, approximately two thirds of D,L\begin{document}$D,L$\end{document} combinations have efficiencies <70%\begin{document}$ \lt 70\%$\end{document}.

caption.title: The performance surface H(D,L)\begin{document}$\boldsymbol{H(D,L)}$\end{document} describes how information capacity changes across all geometrically permissible configurations of an eye of fixed cost – the eye’s morphospace.
caption.p: Red area: high-performance zone in which capacity is >95% maximum. Surfaces plotted for model fly neural superposition (NS) eyes, with F-number, F=2\begin{document}$F=2$\end{document} and total cost, Ctot=4×109\begin{document}$C_{\text{tot}} = 4 \times 10^{9}$\end{document} μm3 sr–1 at three values of photoreceptor energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}. Acceptance angle Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document} calculated using Snyder, 1979 CoG approximation. Data for all graphs is provided in Figure 3—source data 1. The Matlab code used to calculate performance surfaces across the morphospaces of the model eyes studied in this paper is publicly available at https://github.com/fjhheras/eyedesign,copy archived at Heras, 2025.

sec.p: The shape of the performance surface depends upon photoreceptor energy cost (Figure 3). Increasing the energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} reduces Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} from the maximum permitted by eye geometry to one quarter of maximum. However, because the ridge is flat-topped, Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} still lies in an extensive high-efficiency zone, within which L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} can be changed threefold and D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} by 40%, while maintaining >95%\begin{document}$ \gt 95\%$\end{document} efficiency (Figure 3). This broad high-efficiency zone is bad news for investigators who set out to establish that eyes are optimised, but good news for insects. An eye region can be adapted for a specific purpose, for example by increasing L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} to increase SNR, without sacrificing more than 5% of a measure of general-purpose vision, information capacity. Thus, this robust region of design space can facilitate evolution by reducing loss of function.

sec.title: Patterns of investment in optimised NS eye models
sec.p: We next establish how, in theory, eye structure and investments in optics and photoreceptor array vary with total investment when efficiency is optimised by maximising information capacity. We run our fly NS eye model with different combinations of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} and for each combination find the values of Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document}, Dopt\begin{document}$D_{opt}$\end{document}, and Δϕopt\begin{document}$\Delta\phi_{opt}$\end{document} that maximise information capacity and the corresponding investments in optics and photoreceptor array, Co\begin{document}$C_{o}$\end{document} and Cph\begin{document}$C_{ph}$\end{document}. For each combination of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} we run two versions of our NS model, one calculates Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document} using CoG (Snyder, 1979) and the other WOM, which produces a narrower Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document} that comes closer to the lens diffraction limit (Stavenga, 2004a). The two versions give similar patterns of investment (Figure 4).

caption.title: When model fly NS eyes are optimised for information capacity, eye structure, eye performance, and the division of resources between optics and photoreceptor array depend on total investment, Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and photoreceptor energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}.
caption.p: (a) Lens diameter D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}; (b) interommatidial angle, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}; and (c) rhabdomere length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}. Note the sudden jump in the curve for CoG KE=0.12\begin{document}$K_{E}=0.12$\end{document}. (d) Optimum information capacity H\begin{document}$H$\end{document} and rhabdomere length L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} at CoG KE=0.12\begin{document}$K_{E}=0.12$\end{document} plotted over the range of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} within which L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} jumps upward. The eightfold increase in L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} has very little effect on the rate of increase of H\begin{document}$H$\end{document}. (e) %Ctot\begin{document}$\%C_{tot}$\end{document} allocated to photoreceptor array. (f) Photoreceptor energy cost as %Ctot\begin{document}$\%C_{tot}$\end{document}. CoG, acceptance angle approximated by convolving Gaussians (Snyder, 1979); WOM, acceptance angle approximated according to wave optics model (Stavenga, 2004a). Key in (b) also applies to (a). Data for all graphs is provided in Figure 4—source data 1.

sec.title: Rhabdomere length is matched to acuity and rhabdomeres are long
sec.p: As Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} increases, Dopt\begin{document}$D_{opt}$\end{document} widens to increase lens contrast transfer and resolving power by reducing Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document}, Δϕopt\begin{document}$\Delta\phi_{opt}$\end{document} narrows to increase spatial resolution, and Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} lengthens to improve SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document}, reaching several hundred microns in larger eyes (Figure 4a–c). Thus, efficient resource allocation can, in theory, explain why NS eye rhabdomeres are long and rhabdomere length increases with spatial acuity. Note that improving the performance of the optical system by approaching the lens diffraction limit to resolving power improves an eye’s performance in full daylight. With the WOM approximation for Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document} the lens diameter D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} required to achieve a given Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document} is decreased, and this allows Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} to decrease (Figure 4b), thereby increasing the spatial resolution achieved by an efficient eye of given total cost.

sec.title: Photoreceptor energy cost changes the structure of an efficient eye
sec.p: Increasing the photoreceptor energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} has little effect on the optimum values of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, but it reduces the optimum value of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, and hence the depth of the photoreceptor array, by as much as 90% (Figure 4c). The optimum value of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} is most sensitive to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} in the range 0.04–0.16 and tends to jump up and down as Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} increases. Careful interrogation of our MATLAB code suggests that coding and rounding errors are not responsible for these jumps. We attribute the jumps to interactions between competing non-linear terms (e.g. the dependence of noise on L0.5\begin{document}$L^{0.5}$\end{document}, information on log(SNR), effective spatial bandwidth on R0.5\begin{document}$R^{0.5}$\end{document}, volume on R3\begin{document}$R^{3}$\end{document}) that shift the optimum across a flat-topped performance surface. This explanation is supported by the observation that these large jumps in optimum L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} have very little effect on the optimum information rate (Figure 4d). The effects of KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} on eye structure are also sensitive to optical performance: increasing KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} has a greater effect on Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} in the more sharply focussed WOM model at all but the highest values of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} (Figure 4c). As expected, increasing KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} reduces the spatial resolution achieved at a given Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} by reducing the resources available for investment in the volume costs Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document} and Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document}.

sec.title: Photoreceptor arrays are allocated more resources than optics
sec.p: When our NS eye models are optimised for information capacity, more than 50% of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} is allocated to photoreceptor arrays over the range Ctot=2×106\begin{document}$C_{tot}=2\times 10^6$\end{document} to 3×1011\begin{document}$3\times10^{11}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1, irrespective of optical effectiveness (CoG cf. WOM) and photoreceptor energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} (Figure 4e). At lower values of KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, the optimum allocation to photoreceptor arrays is strongly dependent on Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, increasing from 45% to 90% Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} over the range Ctot=1×106\begin{document}$C_{tot}=1\times 10^6$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 to 1×1010\begin{document}$1\times10^{10}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1, over the range and then jumping down to approximately 60% between Ctot=1×1010\begin{document}$C_{tot}=1\times 10^{10}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 and Ctot=1×1011\begin{document}$C_{tot}=1\times 10^{11}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 (Figure 4e). At higher values of KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, the optimum photoreceptor allocation is relatively insensitive to Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and lies between 60% Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and 65% Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. We conclude that, in theory, the cost of the photoreceptor array is as significant as the cost of optics. Therefore, to fully understand eye design, both costs must be taken into account.

The relative contributions of photoreceptor array volume, Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document}, and photoreceptor energy cost, SE\begin{document}$S_{E}$\end{document}, to total array cost, Cph\begin{document}$C_{ph}$\end{document}, depends on total investment, Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} (Figure 4f). At our lowest Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, that is in the lowest acuity eyes regions, the energy cost is 25–50% of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, depending on KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, but as eye size increases the costs associated with array volume come to dominate and photoreceptor energy cost drops to 5% of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}.

sec.title: Flies invest efficiently in costly photoreceptor arrays
sec.p: Our new method for calculating costs enables us to estimate, for the first time, the total investment an animal makes in an eye or eye region, and break this down into its two major components, optics and photoreceptor array. Using published measurements of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, and L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} we determine the specific volumes of dioptric apparatus, Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document}, and photoreceptor array, Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} in fly NS eyes. Although numerous publications give values of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} (Land, 1997; Feller et al., 2021), very few include L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}. We have values for just 12 eye regions, taken from 7 species of flies (Table 2). For each region, we insert the values, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, and f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document}, into Equations 1 and 5 to calculate Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document}, Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document}, and Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. Where focal distance f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document} is not reported (Table 2), we follow our models (Equation 2) and calculate f′=FDni\begin{document}$f^{\prime}=FDn_{i}$\end{document} with F=2\begin{document}$F=2$\end{document} and ni=1.34\begin{document}$n_{i}=1.34$\end{document}.

caption.title: Measurements of Dipteran neural superposition (NS) eyes, extracted from published sources (Land, 1997; Hardie, 1985; Stavenga et al., 1990; Stavenga, 2003a; Land and Eckert, 1985; Stavenga, 2003b; Gonzalez-Bellido et al., 2011; Zeil, 1983; Wardill et al., 2017).
caption.p: Further details of measurements made, their use and results obtained are given in Supplementary file 1.

tr.td: Land, 1997; Hardie, 1985; Stavenga et al., 1990; Stavenga, 2003a

sec.p: The 12 eye regions provide a well-distributed set of empirical values of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, Vo\begin{document}$V_{o}$\end{document}, Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document}, and Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} that spans almost five orders of magnitude of Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, from 1.4×106\begin{document}$1.4\times 10^6$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 and 2.1×106\begin{document}$2.1\times 10^6$\end{document} μm3 sr−1for the low acuity eye region of the March fly Dilophus febrilis and the eye of 9×1010\begin{document}$9\times10^{10}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 Drosophila melanogaster, respectively, to for the remarkable fovea of the small robber fly Holcocephala fusca (Wardill et al., 2017).

We compare the empirical values obtained from flies with theoretical curves, obtained from model NS eyes that are optimised for information capacity (Figure 5). Note that for our theoretical curves, we plot D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, and Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} against total specific cost, Ctotµ⁢m3sr-1\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} (Figure 4). This total includes the surcharge for photoreceptor energy consumption SEµ⁢m3sr-1\begin{document}$S_{E}$\end{document}, which increases in proportion to the number of microvilli and the photoreceptor energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} (Equation 8). However, we cannot estimate Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} in flies because their values of KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} are not well-determined (Methods). Although KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} will vary considerably between species, we only have enough published data to calculate its order of magnitude and likely range (Methods). Furthermore, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} is species-specific and, because we have no more than two data points per species and we are using a generic model that does not account for differences in drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document} and F\begin{document}$F$\end{document} among species (Table 2), we cannot estimate KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} by fitting our model to the empirical data. For these reasons, we compare theoretical findings to empirical data by plotting them against a measure of eye size that is both calculated by our models and estimated from data, total specific volume, Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. Because KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} is uncertain, we plot theoretical curves calculated with values of KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} from within our likely range.

caption.title: Parameters defining the configurations of 12 fly NS eye regions, taken from 7 species (Table 2), compared to model NS eyes optimised for information capacity at given values of photoreceptor energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, and total specific volume Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}.
caption.p: (a) Lens diameter D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, (b) interommatidial angle Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, (c) eye parameter p=DΔϕ\begin{document}$p=D\Delta\phi$\end{document} , and (d) rhabdomere length L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}. (e) Log–log plot of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} vs D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, dashed line shows slope for isomorphic scaling, L∝D\begin{document}$L\propto D$\end{document}. (f) Specific volume of photoreceptor array, Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} expressed as % total specific volume of eye, %Vtot\begin{document}$\%V_{tot}$\end{document}. Models use Snyder’s CoG approximation for photoreceptor acceptance angle (Snyder, 1979). Data for all graphs is provided in Figure 5—source data 1.

sec.p: Looking across 12 fly eye regions, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} increases and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} decreases as Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} increases. D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} lies within, or close to, the theoretical envelope defined by CoG models optimised with KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} = 0 and KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} = 0.32 (Figure 5a), and tends to slightly exceed optimum values at lower specific volumes. Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} is approximately 25% wider than optimum (Figure 5b) in the four eye regions with lowest specific volume and close to optimum elsewhere. The eye parameter, p=DΔϕ\begin{document}$p=D\Delta\phi$\end{document} (Figure 5c) indicates the degree to which an apposition compound eye sacrifices spatial resolution by sampling below the lens diffraction limit. Only Holcocephala fovea, p=0.37\begin{document}$p=0.37$\end{document}, comes close to the diffraction limit p=0.29\begin{document}$p=0.29$\end{document} (Snyder, 1979). As observed in a number of diurnal apposition eyes (Wehner, 1981), the remaining 11 fly eye regions in our data set undersample by a factor of 2 or more, with p\begin{document}$p$\end{document} decreasing with increasing Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} from 1.4 to 0.65 (Figure 5c). Our optimised models also undersample, with p\begin{document}$p$\end{document} decreasing with increasing Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} from 0.89 to 0.65 (Figure 5c). Note that like D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, p\begin{document}$p$\end{document} is relatively insensitive to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}. We conclude that in most eye regions the eye parameter p\begin{document}$p$\end{document} is not optimised for spatio-temporal information capacity, at least according to our model, because it is higher than predicted at all but the highest values of Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}.

Rhabdomere length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, increases with Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} from 60 μm to 340 μm (Figure 5d; Table 2), within or close to the theoretical envelope defined by KE=0\begin{document}$K_{E}=0$\end{document} and KE=0.16\begin{document}$K_{E}=0.16$\end{document}. There is a robust trend: the empirical values of log L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} increase with log Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, and hence with increasing D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and decreasing Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} (Figure 5a, b), along a trajectory that resembles theoretical curves for optimum log L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}. However, the data do not follow the theoretical curve for any particular value of KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, and there is some scatter among the data points (Figure 5d). We have three reasons to attribute most of this scatter to unknown differences in KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}. First, the optimum value of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} is sensitive to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} (Figure 4c); second, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} will vary between species (Methods); and third, the empirical values of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, which are in theory relatively insensitive to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, are less scattered and lie closer to our theoretical predictions (Figure 5a, b). The one obvious outlier, the shorter than predicted rhabdomeres in Holcocephala fovea (Figure 5d), is easily explained: the optics and photoreceptor array of this forward pointing fovea, together with underlying optic lobe, are tightly packed into a head that is less than 600 μm from front to back (Wardill et al., 2017), and this lack of headroom limits the depth of the eye, (L+f′\begin{document}$L+f^{\prime}$\end{document}).

The log–log plot of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} vs D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} (Figure 5e) shows that the increase in L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} with Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} is not explained by proportional scaling because the empirical values do not lie on a straight line of slope 1, L=kD\begin{document}$L={\rm k}D$\end{document}. As expected of a physiological system subject to several competing constraints (Taylor and Thomas, 2014), the theoretical curve continuously changes slope, and this is reflected in the data. Thus, the matching of L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} to D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} observed in the data is not the consequence of a simple developmental constraint; it is the product of developmental mechanisms that allocate resources efficiently to optics and photoreceptor array.

We conclude that the eye regions we analyse are, according to our generic model, not optimised for spatio-temporal information capacity because the data points that are least sensitive to the photoreceptor energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, namely D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} and p\begin{document}$p$\end{document}, are generally larger than predicted. Nonetheless, we argue on two grounds that the matching of rhabdomere length to spatial resolution seen in flies promotes the efficiency of their total investments in eye. First, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} lies within, or close to, the theoretical envelope predicted by models optimised for efficiency and second, because the theoretical performance surface has a wide flat high-efficiency zone (Figure 3), large deviations from optimum have relatively little impact on efficiency.

To accommodate photoreceptors’ lengthy rhabdomeres, flies allocate between 56% and 78% of eye volume, Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, to photoreceptor arrays (Figure 5f). The smaller remainder, between 44% and 22%, is allocated to optics. There is a slight trend for the percentage allocated to photoreceptors to increase with Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. With the exception of Holcocephala’s fovea, where the depth of the photoreceptor array is much less than predicted for optimum allocation, the volumes allocated to photoreceptor arrays lie within or close to the theoretical envelope defined by KE=0\begin{document}$K_{E}=0$\end{document} and KE=0.16\begin{document}$K_{E}=0.16$\end{document}. On this basis, we conclude that our diurnal flies allocate a larger fraction of eye volume to photoreceptors than to optics, and this allocation promotes the efficient use of resources. Thus, photoreceptor costs are playing a major role in determining the design and efficiency of flies’ NS eyes.

sec.title: Fused rhabdom apposition eyes show similar patterns of investment
sec.p: We investigate diurnal apposition eyes with fused rhabdoms by modifying our NS eye model and comparing theoretical results with empirical data using the procedures developed for NS eyes. The modifications are straightforward. We increase lens F-number to 5.5 and reduce drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document} to 1.8μm. These values are in the middle of the wide range found in our sample of apposition eye regions (Table 3). We also adjust the relationship between L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} and the number of microvilli Nvil\begin{document}$N_{vil}$\end{document} to account for a fused rhabdom (Methods). For simplicity, we assume that all photoreceptors contribute equal numbers of microvilli along the full length of the rhabdom so that Nvil\begin{document}$N_{vil}$\end{document} increases in proportion to the depth of the photoreceptor array.

caption.title: Measurements of fused-rhabdom apposition eyes, extracted from published sources (Rossel, 1979; Labhart and Nilsson, 1995; Menzel et al., 1991; Varela and Wiitanen, 1970; Kelber and Somanathan, 2019).
caption.p: Further details of measurements made, their use and results obtained are given in Supplementary file 1.

tr.td: Varela and Wiitanen, 1970; Kelber and Somanathan, 2019

sec.p: We then compare the models’ predictions of the optimum values of L,D,Δϕ,Vo,Vph\begin{document}$L,D,\Delta\phi,V_{o},V_{ph}$\end{document}, and Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} with empirical values extracted from published measurements of D,Δϕ,L\begin{document}$D,\Delta\phi,L$\end{document}, and f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document} (Table 3). The necessary data is only available from studies of four species: the mantid Tenodera australis (Rossel, 1979), the honeybee Apis mellifera (Kelber and Somanathan, 2019; Menzel et al., 1991; Varela and Wiitanen, 1970), and two species of the Libellulid dragonfly Sympetrum that are so similar that their data are lumped together (Labhart and Nilsson, 1995). The studies provide empirical values for 16 eye regions, which span a range of Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, from 4.4×107\begin{document}$4.4\times {10}^{7}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 for Apis worker ventral eye to 1.8×1011\begin{document}$1.8 \times 10^{11} $\end{document} μm3 sr−1 for Sympetrum dorsal fovea (Figure 6).

caption.title: Theoretical results from apposition eye models optimised for information capacity compared to empirical data from 16 apposition eye regions taken from 3 species (Table 3).
caption.p: (a) D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} vs specific volume Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. (b) Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} vs Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. (c) L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} vs Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. (d) photoreceptor array specific volume Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} as %Vtot\begin{document}$\%V_{tot}$\end{document} vs Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. (e, f) Models demonstrate impact of photoreceptor costs and its dependence on total cost, Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. (e) Photoreceptor cost, Cph\begin{document}$C_{ph}$\end{document}, as %Ctot\begin{document}$\%C_{tot}$\end{document}. (f) Photoreceptor energy cost as %Cph\begin{document}$\%C_{ph}$\end{document}. Models run with F-number F=5.5\begin{document}$F=5.5$\end{document}, using COG approximation for acceptance angle (Snyder, 1979) and values of energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} given in keys. Data for all graphs is provided in Figure 6—source data 1.

sec.p: Looking across our limited sample of fused rhabdom apposition eyes, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} increases with Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} decreases, and L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} increases from 280 μm to 1100 μm (Figure 6a–c). These empirical values are more widely scattered than those for NS eyes (Figure 5a, b, d), and the discrepancies between empirical and predicted values are larger. We expect a wider scatter because our sample of fused rhabdom eyes is more diverse than our sample of NS eyes, both structurally and phylogenetically. In addition, the scatter is dominated by regional differences within eyes (Figure 6), several of which adapt a region for a specific task. For example, the 1100 μm rhabdomeres of the dragonfly Sympetrum, which are the longest photoreceptive waveguides found in any eye, adapt the fovea for detecting small prey against a bright blue sky by enhancing SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document} (Labhart and Nilsson, 1995). Despite the scatter in data points, fused rhabdom eyes follow the trends seen in NS eyes (Figure 6 cf. Figure 5). In most eye regions, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document} and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} are larger than predicted by models optimised for efficiency (Figure 6a, b), so therefore is the eye parameter p\begin{document}$p$\end{document} (values not plotted). At low Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} lies within the theoretical envelope defined by models run with plausible values of photoreceptor energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}. However, at higher specific volumes L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} falls increasingly short (Figure 6c). As with flies’ NS eyes, large investments are made in photoreceptor arrays. The volume fractions of photoreceptor arrays increase from 33% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} to 75% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} as Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} increases, and the majority of values lie between 40% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} and 60% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} (Figure 6d). Our information maximisation models predict an increase from 33% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} to 85% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}.

Our models also give the relative contribution of photoreceptor cost to total cost when fused rhabdom apposition eyes are optimised for spatial information capacity. The percentage of total investment devoted to the photoreceptor array increases with the energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} and climbs steadily with increasing Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, from 43% to more than 80% (Figure 6e). As in NS eyes, the contribution of photoreceptor energy consumption to array cost is progressively marginalised by array volume cost as Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} increases, falling to less than 20% in the largest eyes (Figure 6f).

We conclude that the 16 eye regions in our sample of fused rhabdom apposition eyes are not optimised for spatio-temporal information capacity, at least according to our models. However, the regions exhibit three trends that are associated with efficient resource allocation – rhabdomeres are relatively long, length increases with acuity, and the photoreceptor array occupies a large fraction of eye volume. These trends suggest that, as in NS eyes, resources are being divided between optics and photoreceptor array to promote efficiency. Consequently, photoreceptor costs play a major role in the design of fused-rhabdom apposition eyes.

sec.title: Patterns of efficient investment are different in simple eyes
sec.p: To further investigate how investment patterns differ according to eye type, we construct a basic hemispherical model of a simple eye with rhabdomeric photoreceptors (Methods; Figure 7a) that captures the equivalence of D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document}, and Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document} in apposition and simple eyes (Kirschfeld, 1976). We then follow the procedures described above to see how optimum patterns of investment depend on total investment, Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, and photoreceptor energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}. We consider the same 5 orders of magnitude of total investment, Ctot=1×106\begin{document}$C_{tot}=1\times 10^{6}$\end{document} to 2.5×1011\begin{document}$2.5\times 10^{11}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1, and for simple eye models this encompasses a 100-fold range of lens diameters, D=\begin{document}$D=$\end{document} 29 μm to 2.9 mm, and focal distances, f′ = 79 µm to 7.9 mm.

caption.title: Resource allocation in model simple eyes optimised for information capacity compared to optimised model neural superposition apposition eyes (NS).
caption.p: (a) Schematic showing apposition eye and simple eye with identical spatial resolution, as defined by lens diameter D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, focal distance f′\begin{document}$f^{\prime}$\end{document}, rhabdom(ere) diameter drh\begin{document}$d_{rh}$\end{document}, and rhabdom(ere) length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} (after Kirschfeld, 1976). Note the denser packing of photoreceptors inside the simple eye. (b–e) Properties of simple and NS eye models optimised for information capacity in full daylight. (b) Photoreceptor length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} vs specific volume Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}; (c) photoreceptor specific volume Vph\begin{document}$V_{ph}$\end{document} expressed as %Vtot\begin{document}$\%V_{tot}$\end{document} vs Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. (d) Photoreceptor investment Cph\begin{document}$C_{ph}$\end{document} as %Ctot\begin{document}$\%C_{tot}$\end{document} vs Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. (e) photoreceptor energy cost as %Ctot\begin{document}$\%C_{tot}$\end{document} vs Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. Models use Snyder, 1979 CoG approximation for acceptance angle. Data for all graphs is provided in Figure 7—source data 1.

sec.p: For each Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, there is an optimum division of resources between optics and photoreceptors that maximises information capacity. Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} increases with Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, scaling close to (Ctot)1/3\begin{document}$\left(C_{tot}\right)^{1/3}$\end{document} (Figure 7b). Increasing the energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, greatly reduces Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} without changing this scaling exponent. At any given Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} and KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}, Lopt\begin{document}$L_{opt}$\end{document} is much shorter in simple eyes and the difference increases with Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} from at least 50% shorter at Ctot=1×106\begin{document}$C_{tot}= 1\times 10^{6}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 to over 90% shorter at Ctot=1×109\begin{document}$C_{tot}=1\times 10^{9}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1. We conclude that, in theory, efficient resource allocation explains why photoreceptors in diurnal simple eyes have much shorter light-sensitive waveguides than photoreceptors in diurnal apposition eyes.

Patterns of investment differ from apposition eyes in other respects (Figure 7). In simple models, the optimum volume fraction of the photoreceptor array is insensitive to Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} whereas in apposition models, it increases with Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. In addition, a simple eye is much more sensitive to photoreceptor energy consumption. The small energy tariff, KE=0.04\begin{document}$K_{E}=0.04$\end{document}, reduces simple eye array volume from 35% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} to 12% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}, but has a negligible effect on an NS eye. Raising the tariff to KE=0.32\begin{document}$K_{E}=0.32$\end{document}, reduces the simple eye’s array volume to just 3% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document} but in apposition models it is always > 30% Vtot\begin{document}$V_{tot}$\end{document}. Despite this tenfold reduction in %Vtot\begin{document}$\%V_{tot}$\end{document} the total resources allocated to photoreceptor array increases from 35% to 57% (Figure 7d) because the relative contribution of photoreceptor energy consumption increases from 40% Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} to 55% Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}. Finally, in simple eyes, the relative contribution of photoreceptor energy consumption is insensitive to Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, but in apposition models, it falls steadily with increasing Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document}, to around 5% Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} (Figure 7d). This difference between eye types can be attributed to differences in eye geometry (Figure 7a). As Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} increases, a simple eye’s photoreceptors maintain their dense packing, but an apposition eye’s photoreceptors become increasingly widely spaced as larger lenses force the boundaries between ommatidia further apart. We conclude that when resources are efficiently allocated, the differences in geometry that define eye type can profoundly influence both the impact of photoreceptor energy consumption on eye morphology and the distribution of costs within an eye.

sec.title: Information capacity and investment
sec.p: In all optimised eye models, apposition and simple, the spatio-temporal information capacity, Hopt\begin{document}$H_{opt}$\end{document}, increases sub-linearly with total investment, Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} (Figure 8a), making a bit of information more expensive in a larger eye. In simple eyes, Hopt\begin{document}$H_{opt}$\end{document} increases as (Ctot)0.8\begin{document}$\left(C_{tot}\right)^{0.8}$\end{document} across the full range of Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} while in apposition models the exponent is lower, decreasing from 0.6 to 0.5 as Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} increases. Thus, the simple eye model is 10 times more efficient when Ctot=1×106\begin{document}$C_{\text{tot}} = 1 \times 10^{6} $\end{document} μm3 sr−1 and approximately 100 times more efficient when Ctot=1×1011\begin{document}$C_{\text{tot}} = 1 \times 10^{11}$\end{document} μm3 sr−1 (Figure 8b). Increasing the photoreceptor energy tariff reduces the efficiency of all eye types, but this effect declines as apposition eye models increase in size (Figure 8b) because, for reasons of geometry, volume costs marginalise photoreceptor energy cost.

caption.title: Information rate, H\begin{document}$H$\end{document} vs total cost Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} for simple and apposition model eyes optimised to maximise information capacity in full daylight.
caption.p: (a) Rates are higher in simple eyes and more sensitive to photoreceptor energy tariff, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document}. (b) Efficiency, bits per unit specific volume per second, falls less steeply with increasing Ctot\begin{document}$C_{tot}$\end{document} in simple eyes. Models use Snyder’s CoG approximation (1979) for acceptance angle. Data for all graphs is provided in Figure 8—source data 1.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: We initiate a new approach to understanding eye design by investigating how the division of resources between an eye’s dioptric apparatus and photoreceptor array influences an eye’s performance and efficiency. To achieve this, we formulate a new measure of cost, specific volume, that is defined by an eye’s shape and the dimensions of its components, can be adjusted to account for the high energy cost of photoreceptors, and relates investments in optics and investments in photoreceptors to three basic determinants of image quality, interreceptor angle Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, photoreceptor acceptance angle Δρ\begin{document}$\Delta\rho$\end{document}, and photoreceptor signal to noise ratio SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document}, via optical, geometrical, and physiological constraints. These advantages allow us to model how an eye’s performance changes when resources are transferred between optics and photoreceptors. Thus, we can, for the first time, model performance across the morphospace of an eye of given type and total cost. Using a general-purpose measure of performance, spatio-temporal information capacity (Howard and Snyder, 1983; van Hateren, 1992), we find that the surface for an apposition compound eye (Figure 3) is flat topped. Thus, the configuration that maximises performance sits in a broad high-efficiency zone in which performance is more than 95% of maximum. Within this robust region of morphospace, an eye, or eye region, can be specialised for a specific task with little loss of information, and this can facilitate evolution by reducing loss of function. Our models predict that efficiently configured diurnal apposition eyes invest heavily in deep photoreceptor arrays with long rhabdomeres and rhabdoms, and match investments in optics and photoreceptor arrays so that rhabdom(ere) length increases with spatial resolution (Figure 4). By applying our new method of costing investments to published data, we show that fast-flying diurnal insects conform to these trends and are, therefore, configured to be efficient (Figures 5 and 6). Our models also demonstrate that photoreceptor energy consumption is an important factor in an apposition eye’s design and performance: it reduces photoreceptor length by as much as 90% and information capacity by as much as 60%. To see how resource allocation and performance depend on eye type, we model a simple (camera type) eye (Figure 7). When optimised for efficiency, a simple eye has much shorter photoreceptors and its structure and function are more sensitive to photoreceptor energy demands: photoreceptor length is greatly reduced by and information capacity decreases by as much as 90% (Figures 7 and 8). We conclude that when apposition and simple eyes are designed to use resources efficiently, the cost of the photoreceptor array is as significant as the cost of optics, and matching investments in optics and photoreceptor arrays to increase efficiency is a principle of eye design. Our analysis, which is the first to account for the costs of both optics and photoreceptor array, indicates that one benefit of investing in an eye, the ability to gather more information, follows the law of diminishing returns. As expected from its superior geometry (Kirschfeld, 1976), a simple eye is in theory a better investment: its information capacity increases as (total cost)0.8, compared with (total cost)0.55 for an apposition eye, and the simple eye is 10–100 times more efficient at gathering information (Figure 8). We now discuss the validity of our methodology, the robustness of our findings, and their contributions to our understanding of eye design and evolution.

sec.title: Specific volume is a useful measure of cost
sec.p: This new measure of cost has several advantages. First, specific volume is defined by dimensions and angles that are measured anatomically and optically (Equations 1, 2, 5, and 6) and relates directly to benefits via optical, geometrical and physiological constraints on spatial acuity and sensitivity. Thus, like previous indicators of cost, namely eye radius, eye length, pupil area, and corneal area (Brandon et al., 2015; Brandon and Dudycha, 2014; Snyder et al., 1977; Thomas et al., 2020; Thomas et al., 2004), specific volume can evaluate investments and trade-offs that adapt eyes for specific purposes. Second, specific volume is more flexible and far-reaching than previous indicators of cost because it can be applied to eyes that are not well approximated by solid spheres. Third, it applies to small eye regions. Fourth, it provides a common currency for comparing investments in different eyes, eye regions, and components within an eye (Figures 5—7). This facility enables one to investigate how and why resources are distributed across visual fields and, as we demonstrate here, evaluate trade-offs between investments in an eye’s components. Given that biological processes and systems evolve to become more cost-efficient because costs depress fitness (Alexander, 1996; Niven and Laughlin, 2008; Taylor and Thomas, 2014), we suggest a fifth advantage: specific volume will help us understand how eyes are adapted and evolve.

How well does specific volume represent costs? Volume accurately measures one limiting resource, space, but because there are insufficient data, we have to make assumptions to calculate the costs of energy and materials. Assuming that the densities of an eye’s cells, extracellular materials and fluid-filled cavities are close to 1, space is a reasonable proxy for three costs that increase in proportion to mass: the energy expended carrying an eye (carriage cost), materials, and cellular energy consumption (Witter and Cuthill, 1993).

Equating mass and carriage cost is a reasonable starting point. Experiments on individual animals – a human walker carrying a backpack (Bastien et al., 2005), a flightless ant carrying nectar (Duncan and Lighton, 1994), and a flying beetle carrying a heavy weapon (Goyens et al., 2015) – show that the cost of carrying an additional load is the load’s mass times the individual’s mass specific metabolic rate when moving unloaded.

Turning to the costs of the materials and energy used within the eye, there is no data for an apposition eye’s dioptric apparatus. Nonetheless, its corneal lenses, pigment cells, and cone cells are densely packed with macromolecules, and because their optical function is passive (they refract and absorb), they contain very few, if any, mitochondria. Indeed, the corneal cuticle and the fluid-filled pseudo-cone of fly NS eye are extracellular (Hardie, 1985). Thus, the assumption that the costs of materials and energy for the dioptric apparatus increase in fixed proportion to volume is a reasonable starting point.

Cost estimates can be adjusted for situations in which costs per unit volume are not equal, as illustrated by our treatment of photoreceptor energy consumption. To support transduction, the photoreceptor array has an exceptionally high metabolic rate (Laughlin et al., 1998; Pangršič et al., 2005; Niven et al., 2007). We account for this higher energy cost by using the animal’s specific metabolic rate (power per unit mass and hence power per unit volume) to convert an array’s power consumption into an equivalent volume (Methods). Photoreceptor ion pumps are the major consumers of energy and the smaller contribution of pigmented glia (Coles, 1989) is included in our calculation of the energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} (Methods). The higher costs of materials and their turnover in the photoreceptor array can be added to the energy tariff KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} but given the magnitude of the light-gated current (Laughlin et al., 1998), the relative increase will be very small. Thus, for our intents and purposes, the effects of these additional costs are covered by our models. For want of sufficient data, KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} is uncertain; therefore, we model a reasonable range of values (Methods). Because the optimum eye configuration is most sensitive to KE\begin{document}$K_{E}$\end{document} in the lower half of this range (Figures 4 and 7), we are unlikely to seriously underestimate the effects of photoreceptor costs on eye morphology and efficiency.

sec.title: Matching photoreceptor length to spatial acuity
sec.p: The systematic increase in rhabdomere or rhabdom length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, with spatial acuity observed in apposition eyes (Figures 1, 5 and 6) and simple eyes (see below), and predicted by our models (Figures 4 and 7), is an obvious manifestation of efficient resource allocation. Two studies of apposition eyes, one of dragonfly and the other of praying mantis (Labhart and Nilsson, 1995; Rossel, 1979), map the covariation of lens diameter, D\begin{document}$D$\end{document}, interommatidial angle, Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, and rhabdom length, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document}, across an entire eye. Both show that when acuity is increased by enlarging the dioptric apparatus and increasing ommatidial density, that is by reducing Δϕ\begin{document}$\Delta\phi$\end{document}, L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} is increased to improve SNRph\begin{document}$SNR_{ph}$\end{document}. In both insects L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} peaks in a fovea that is used to detect and capture prey, and in dragonfly dorsal fovea the 1.1mm\begin{document}$1.1\,\mathrm{mm}$\end{document} rhabdomeres (the longest light sensitive waveguides reported in any eye, diurnal or nocturnal) are adapted to improve the detection and localisation of small flying prey (Labhart and Nilsson, 1995; Rigosi et al., 2017). However, specialisation for prey capture is unlikely to explain why L\begin{document}$L$\end{document} varies systematically with acuity across an entire eye.

We find that the trend to increase rhabdomere length with acuity is more generally advantageous.
